Victorian eye portrait — illustration by the authorA brief architectural synopsis and integration guide
This article builds on A Hybrid Refresh Token System, which introduced identity hashing as a stateless way to enforce global identity invalidation. In this follow‑up, we integrate that identity‑hash model with a session table to add server‑side per‑session lifecycle control: invalidating a specific device from the server, detecting stolen refresh tokens, and tracking active sessions. The goal is to show how session state can be layered on top of identity state without losing the clean semantics, predictability, or privacy properties of the original hybrid model.
1. Purpose of Combining the Two Models
Combining the hybrid identity‑hash model with a session table creates a layered structure:

• Identity state → global, user‑wide

• Session state → per‑device, per‑login

• Refresh token → carries both identity and session information
This preserves stateless identity semantics while adding server-side session control where needed.
2. Core Data Model
User Record
Contains fields that define identity state, e.g.:

• password hash

• email

• role

• MFA flags

• any other identity‑relevant fields
Identity hash = hash of these fields.
Session Table
One row per login/register event:

• id (session_id)

• user_id

• session_counter (monotonic integer)

• optional timestamps (created_at, last_refreshed_at)

• optional metadata (IP, UA, device label)
A session row represents a single device’s long‑lived session.
Refresh Token Payload
Carries:

• user_id

• identity_hash

• session_id

• session_counter
This allows the server to validate both identity and session state on every refresh.
3. Behavioral Invariants
Identity Invariant (Global)
If the recomputed identity hash ≠ token’s identity hash →
All sessions for that user must be invalidated.
Correct action:

• delete all session rows where user_id = token.user_id

• clear refresh cookie (force login)
Triggered by: password change, email change, role change, MFA changes, etc.
Session Invariant (Per‑Session)
If the session row is missing or its counter ≠ token’s counter →
Only that session is invalid.
Correct action:

• delete that one session row

• clear refresh cookie (force login)
Triggered by: server-side logout of one device, stolen token detection, session expiration.
These invariants remain cleanly separated.
4. Handler Integration Guide
registerHandler

• Create user

• Create new session row

• Issue refresh token containing: (user_id, identity_hash, session_id, session_counter=0).

• Each registration creates a new session, never reusing an old one.
loginHandler

• Authenticate user

• Create new session row

• Issue refresh token containing: (user_id, identity_hash, session_id, session_counter=0).

• Each login creates a new session, never reusing an old one.
refreshHandler
This is the core of the integration:
Extract the user_id, identity_hash, session_id, and session_counter from token.
Recompute identity hash from current user record
A mismatch means global invalidation, so…

• delete all session rows for this user_id

• clear refresh cookie (force login)
Load the corresponding session row
A missing session row means session invalidation, so…

• clear refresh cookie (force login)
Compare the session_counter
A mismatch means possible refresh token theft, so

• delete only this session row

• clear refresh cookie (force login)
Rotate the refresh token

• increment session_counter

• update session row

• issue a new refresh token with the same user_id, identity_hash, session_id and the updated session_counter
Refresh never creates a new session.
It always preserves the session_id.
logoutHandler (logout one device)

• Delete the session row for the current session_id

• clear refresh cookie (force login)
Only that device is logged out.
logoutAllHandler (logout all devices)

• Increment token-version for the current user-id

• Delete all session rows for the user
Invalidates all identity-bound tokens (refresh tokens, password reset tokens). All devices for the user are logged-out.
deleteUserHandler

• Delete all session rows

• Delete user
5. What a Session Table Adds to the Hybrid Model
Benefits

• Stolen token detection

• Active session tracking

• Provide “logged in devices” UI

• Invalidate a compromised session from the server without global logout

• Optional device metadata for operators
Costs

• DB writes on every refresh

• Cleanup jobs for expired sessions

• More synchronization in logout/delete flows

• More UI edge cases (e.g., user forced to log in on one device but not others)

• Slightly more cognitive load for developers
The hybrid model works without a session table, but the table adds capabilities some products need.
Privacy Properties of the Hybrid Model
The hybrid identity‑hash + session‑table model supports long‑lived sessions while storing only the minimum information required to validate them. This aligns with the data minimization principle, which states that a system should collect and retain only the data strictly necessary for its purpose.
In this architecture:

• the refresh token is the session

• A minimal session table only requires: session_id, user_id, and session_counter.

• no device identity is required to validate a session

• no IP address, MAC address, browser fingerprint, or machine identifier is needed for correctness
Optional metadata (such as IP or user‑agent) may be stored for operator UX, but it is not part of the security model and is not required for the system to function.
Because session validity depends solely on:

• identity_hash (global identity state)

• session_id (per‑session state)

• session_counter (rotation state)
…the system avoids cross‑device tracking entirely. There is no implicit device identity, no behavioral profiling, and no hidden correlation layer.
This makes the hybrid model a strong match for jurisdictions that emphasize privacy and the data minimization principle, because the architecture naturally limits what the server must store. By collecting only the minimum information required to validate a session, this architecture proves you don’t need to track users at all to achieve long‑lived sessions, global invalidation, and theft detection.
6. Why Identity Hash and Session State Should Stay Separate
Identity hash = global identity state
Session table = per‑session state
If you mix session fields into the identity hash:

• each session gets a unique identity hash

• changing one session invalidates only that session

• but you lose the ability to distinguish identity invalidation from session invalidation

• and you lose the ability to globally invalidate all sessions cleanly
Keeping them separate preserves clean semantics and predictable behavior.
7. Summary
A session table integrates cleanly with the hybrid identity‑hash model when:

• identity hash handles global invalidation

• session table handles per‑session invalidation

• refresh tokens carry both identity_hash and session_id

• refresh rotates the session counter but never creates new sessions

• login/register create new sessions

• logout/delete remove session rows

• identity mismatch deletes all session rows for that user
Scenario: Attacker steals a refresh token and refreshes first
Initial state
User has a valid session row:

• session_id = S1, session_counter = N
User’s refresh token contains:

• (identity_hash, S1, N)

• Attacker steals that token.
Step 1 — Attacker calls /auth/refresh first
The attacker presents the stolen token:

• Identity hash matches → identity is still valid

• Session row exists → session is still valid

• Session counter matches → no theft detected (yet)
So the attacker receives:

• a new refresh token with (identity_hash, S1, N+1)

• the session row is updated to session_counter = N+1
Attacker now has a live, extended session.
This is expected — theft detection requires two parties attempting to refresh the same session.
Step 2 — Legitimate user calls /auth/refresh
The user presents their old refresh token:

• Their token still contains (identity_hash, S1, N)

• But the session row now contains session_counter = N+1
So the refresh handler sees:

• identity hash matches → identity is still valid

• session row exists → session is still valid

• session_counter mismatch → theft detected
Correct response:

• delete the session row for S1

• clear the refresh cookie

• return Unauthorized
The response forces both attacker and legitimate user to login.
Step 3 — What happens to the attacker now?
The attacker still holds a refresh token with:

• (identity_hash, S1, N+1)
But the session row for S1 has been deleted.
So on their next refresh attempt:

• identity hash matches

• session row missing → session invalid

• refresh fails

• attacker is logged out
This is the correct and intended outcome.
Step 4 — Legitimate user logs in again
User performs a fresh login:

• A new session row is created: session_id = S2, session_counter = 0

• User receives a new refresh token with (identity_hash, S2, 0)
This cleanly isolates the new session from the compromised one.
Does the attacker have access between Step 1 and Step 2?
Yes — temporarily.
This is inherent to any refresh‑token‑based theft‑detection model that relies on:

• session counters

• token rotation

• mismatch detection
The attacker gets a window of access until:

• the legitimate user refreshes, or

• the legitimate user changes identity state (password/email/role), or

• the session expires naturally
This is the same behavior seen in:

• OAuth 2.0 token rotation

• Google’s session theft detection

• Auth0’s refresh token rotation

• AWS Cognito’s refresh token rotation
It’s the standard “race to refresh” model.
